explicit_text: I was born in Los Angeles, California, and lived there until I was nine years old. I remember being told I was born on a sunny Saturday in April at noon, which really made me feel like a California kid. My early childhood memories are rooted in that city before my family moved to London.

explicit_text: When I was nine, my family moved to London, England, where I grew up until I was 15. Those years in London were formative before I moved back to the United States to finish high school. It was quite a change from Los Angeles, but London became my home during those important adolescent years.

explicit_text: I chose to go the ROTC route at Tufts University because it guaranteed I would end up in the Marine Corps, unlike the Naval Academy which could have sent me to the Navy. ROTC also gave me the flexibility to pursue extra military training during summers, like jump school and amphibious reconnaissance. It was a key part of my development as a Marine officer before I even graduated college.

explicit_text: I graduated and was commissioned in May 2003, just after the Iraq invasion had begun in March and April. I went to Marine Corps Basic School during a time when the war was initially thought to be over, but by the time I finished, it was clear Marines were heading into intense combat operations in Iraq. The war shaped my early career and the training I received.

entity: United States Marine Corps Basic School
explicit_text: I checked into the United States Marine Corps Basic School in June 2003, right after the Iraq invasion. Initially, there was a feeling that the war was over, but by the time I graduated that Christmas, it was clear we were heading into combat. The Basic School was where I was designated an infantry officer and prepared for deployment.

explicit_text: In August 2003, the Marine Corps Times ran a front-page story titled 'The Marines Back to Iraq,' which marked a turning point in how we viewed our upcoming deployment. It was a time when many of us were realizing that combat was imminent, changing the atmosphere at Basic School and beyond.

explicit_text: I remember seeing a Washington Post front page photo of Marines carrying a comrade in a body bag through the streets of Ramadi. This was a stark reminder of the dangers we faced, and it was the first time Marine officers graduating Basic School were certain they were going into combat. Ramadi was a focal point of intense fighting during that period.

explicit_text: Paul Felsberg was a friend of mine who was a prior enlisted Marine and graduated early from Basic School to replace a fallen Marine officer named Reblowski. Sadly, Felsberg was killed in Ramadi in October during the intense fighting. His story personalized the cost of the war for me and my classmates.

explicit_text: I was in college when the September 11 attacks happened, and it was a turning point for me and many others. Before 9/11, my older brother thought I wouldn't see combat, but after the attacks, the likelihood of war became very real. That event fundamentally changed how I viewed my military career and the possibility of deployment.
